leads.PEST_PROBLEM: Customer called Caster pest control regarding a mouse problem in their upstairs condo unit near the dishwasher and oven area. The customer has set multiple traps (bucket traps and steel traps) without success over the past two days and expressed concern about committing to a one-year service contract, noting this is their first rodent issue in 18 years and suspecting the mouse is entering from outside/below rather than being a result of poor housekeeping. Agent Amanda explained that Caster only offers one-year maintenance contracts following an initial inspection and quote, and could not provide pricing information without an inspection first. The customer decided to call back after checking with other pest control companies in the Waterbury area, as they were uncertain about committing to a year-long service.

The caller claimed to represent multiple copier manufacturers (Xerox, Canon, Sharp, Konica, Kyocera) and asked Rusty to check either a color or black and white copier. The call was transferred to Sam before a resolution could be reached, and no model number information was provided.

Bevin Bankston called to schedule a pest control inspection for his newly purchased four-unit multi-family property located at 9 Nickerson Street, Pawtucket, Rhode Island 02860. Two of the small studio units (approximately 300 square feet each) have severe roach infestations, while the other two occupied units have no complaints; the vacant units have been empty since December with minimal activity observed after recent renovations. Agent Kevin explained that the entire building would need to be inspected and treated since roaches can travel between units through walls, and offered a free inspection with a 2:30-3:30 PM arrival window for tomorrow (the 22nd), though Bevin requested to call back to confirm the appointment after coordinating with his tenant and wife due to scheduling constraints with a newborn at home. The call ended with Bevin agreeing to call back to finalize the inspection date and time.

leads.PEST_PROBLEM: Customer called Cat's Eye to inquire about bat removal services for approximately 12-14 bats living in their exterior shutters for the past two years. Agent Kevin explained that the company only handles bat evictions if they are inside the home (such as in the attic), and cannot assist with bats in exterior shutters since they are a protected species. Customer was advised to try deterrence methods or consider removing the shutters entirely, and was told to call back if bats enter the attic.

A new homeowner called Cat's Eye pest control to inquire about removing a gopher from their backyard shed. Agent Amanda explained that Cat's Eye does not offer trapping and removal services for outdoor animals due to inconsistent results, but can seal structures to prevent entry. Amanda suggested the customer contact smaller local companies that specialize in cage trapping, as larger companies typically don't offer this service. The customer accepted this explanation and ended the call satisfied with the referral guidance.

Andre Martin, a property owner of a duplex in Middletown (392 Millbrook Road, 06457), called to schedule a pest inspection after his tenant reported seeing a rat or mouse near the front entrance and kitchen. Rusty explained Cat's Eye Pest Control's process, which includes a thorough 45-minute to 1-hour inspection followed by ongoing treatment plans that cover rodents and other pests, with pricing determined after the inspection. An appointment was scheduled for Thursday, the 23rd at 8:30 AM with inspector Vinny to meet the tenant Michaela, and Andre will attempt to gain access to the other unit as well to prevent mice from migrating between sides of the duplex.

leads.PEST_PROBLEM: Customer Ed Clausen called on behalf of his in-laws in Andover who have a mouse infestation problem, primarily in the basement and kitchen. The issue stems from the father-in-law's practice of live-trapping and releasing mice outside near the house, causing them to return, and is complicated by the father-in-law's recent hospitalization for pneumonia and the mother-in-law's recovery from COVID, making the health risks of the infestation a concern. Agent Amanda explained Catseye's approach of using snap traps with weekly maintenance visits for 4-6 weeks followed by a monthly or bi-monthly maintenance program, and clarified that unlike competitors, they use physical trapping methods rather than loading houses with bait. Ed agreed to discuss the situation with his siblings and indicated he would follow up within a couple of days, with Amanda noting she would contact him for a follow-up if needed.

Agent Rusty explained that the company does not use poisons inside dwellings as rodents die in walls and create odor, and advised that treating only the second floor would push mice downstairs, requiring landlord coordination for a building-wide solution. Agent recommended sealing entry points with steel wool around utility lines, radiators, and other gaps rather than relying on traps alone, and advised the customer to inform their landlord to address these vulnerabilities before receiving further rent payments. The customer expressed appreciation and indicated they would follow up with their landlord on sealing entry points.

There’s something else I’d like to contact you about. Hi,
My name is Javier, and I’m a museum educator at the Harvard Museum of Natural History. I develop hands-on activities and programs for children and families, many of which focus on insects.
I’m reaching out to see if you might be able to help with an upcoming activity centered on bees and wasps. As part of this program, participants explore the structure of hexagonal hives by using dried wasp nests and honeycombs as stamps. It’s a fun way to connect science and art, but the materials tend to wear out quickly, and we’ve run out.
I’m contacting local pest management companies to ask if you would be willing to collect and set aside any intact or usable hives you encounter during your work. This could include paper wasp nests, bald-faced hornet nests, bee hives, or similar structures. I’d be happy to arrange pickup or cover shipping to the museum.
These materials will be used solely for educational programming and will help provide meaningful, hands-on learning experiences for our community.
If you’re unable to assist but know someone who might be, I would greatly appreciate you passing this message along.
Thank you very much for your time and consideration—I hope to hear from you soon.
Best regards,
Javier
